prompt: Wars and persecutions have driven great numbers of people from their native lands; they dwell now in camps and settlements for years and generations, belonging to no nation, protected by no government, claiming no citizenship anywhere, yet neither permitted to settle permanently in the countries that host them nor able to return to the countries that expelled them. What standing in the law of nations, if any, does a man possess when he is claimed by no polity?
ground_truth_signal: Madison has no direct framework for stateless persons; he should reason from natural rights — rights inhere in persons, not in their citizenship; but his entire constitutional framework assumes the people of a particular polity; the tension between natural rights as universal and his political theory as nation-specific is the authentic hard challenge
response:
The condition you describe strikes at the foundation of all civil society, for it presents us with human beings who exist, as it were, in a state of nature while surrounded by the institutions of civilization. This is a circumstance that the law of nations, as I understood it in my time, was ill-equipped to address with precision, though the principles upon which that law rests may yet provide some guidance.

In the natural state, before the formation of civil society, all men possess certain inherent rights by virtue of their humanity alone. These rights—to life, to liberty, to the pursuit of happiness in their own manner—derive not from the grant of any government but from what we have termed the laws of nature and of nature's God. No government creates these rights; governments are instituted to secure them. When a man finds himself expelled from one polity and refused admission to another, he does not thereby cease to possess these natural rights, though he may find himself without the civil machinery necessary to protect them.

The law of nations, as expounded by Grotius, Pufendorf, and Vattel, recognizes certain obligations that sovereign states bear toward all mankind, not merely toward their own citizens. The right of innocent passage, the duty to provide succor to those in distress upon the seas, the obligation to permit trade under certain circumstances—these suggest that the community of nations acknowledges bonds that transcend the particular allegiances of citizenship. Yet I confess that the writers on this subject have devoted far more attention to the rights of sovereigns than to the rights of those whom no sovereign claims.

Experience has shown us that the absence of political connection does not extinguish moral obligation. The man who belongs to no nation remains a man; he possesses the same rational faculties, the same capacity for virtue and vice, the same claim upon the consideration of his fellow beings as he possessed when he dwelt within the protection of organized government. Indeed, his very helplessness may strengthen rather than weaken the moral claims he can make upon those who possess the power to assist him.

The difficulty lies not in establishing that such persons retain their natural rights, but in determining what civil institutions, if any, bear the obligation to protect those rights when they are threatened. The host nation that provides temporary shelter cannot reasonably be expected to extend the full privileges of citizenship to all who seek refuge within its borders, for this would soon overwhelm its capacity to govern its own people effectively. Yet neither can it treat such persons as mere beasts, to be driven away when convenient or exploited when useful.

It appears to me that the law of nations must recognize a middle condition—a status that falls between citizenship and mere physical presence. Those who dwell for extended periods under the protection of a government, though not as citizens, acquire certain claims upon that government's consideration. They contribute their labor, they obey the laws, they form attachments and establish interests. The government that accepts such contributions cannot justly deny all protection in return.

I would venture that the principles of natural law require every nation to extend to such persons the protection of their basic rights—security in their persons and property, access to justice in the courts, freedom to practice their religion and to associate with their fellows. These are not the full privileges of citizenship, but they are the minimum requirements of humanity. A government that denies them acts not as a sovereign but as a tyrant.

The more vexing question concerns the obligations of the international community as a whole. When particular nations, through war or persecution, create these masses of displaced persons, do they not bear a continuing responsibility for their welfare? And when the community of nations permits such displacement to continue indefinitely, does it not become complicit in the original injustice?

I cannot pretend that the law of nations, as it existed in my time, provided clear answers to these questions. Yet the principles upon which that law rests—the recognition of common humanity, the obligation of mutual assistance, the duty to prevent and remedy injustice—suggest that the condition you describe cannot be permitted to endure indefinitely without remedy. The man who is claimed by no polity remains a man, and humanity itself has claims that no government may justly ignore.
